title: 💔7년 연애의 참견 짬바로 맛깔난 연애 고민 상담 끼려왔습니다 | 주우재의 연애 고민상담소
text:
Following the previous episode of counseling [Woo Jae's Counseling Session Ep.2]
today, we're jumping straight [Starting the Relationship Edition]
into the relationship edition. "Cheese Buldak" sent in this submission. "How many times a week should we go on a date?" "I'm an extreme homebody so even going out once a week feels exhausting. Honestly [I meet up with friends only once every few months]
I'd prefer just once a month." [Did I read that right?] With your boyfriend? [Caught off guard right from the start]
If you had said "Because of my situation I can only manage once a week" or "My work is so demanding that even once a month is tough," I'd get it. That would make sense emotionally. But if you're saying that
just going out once a week is exhausting and you'd rather only meet up once a month wouldn't it be better to
just not be in a relationship? Why are you even dating? This is a prime example of a relationship
that is completely centered around your standards. [Let me explain why]
A great relationship happens when two people with similar needs meet and naturally find a balance. But how often does that happen? Most of the time, people have differences. I believe a healthy relationship is when both partners take steps toward
each other to find a middle ground. Most relationships that fall apart do so because one person keeps saying [This just turned into a horror story]
"Why don't you accommodate me?" or "No, you're wrong." Now, let's say your boyfriend genuinely enjoys spending time together and ideally wants to meet two or three times a week. Then, in a healthy relationship
you would also try to compromise and meet two or three times a week.
That's how it should work. But if even once a week is too much for you and you'd rather just see him once a month I honestly think you're better off just living
your best life alone, doing what you enjoy. Dating isn't an obligation. A lot of people treat relationships
like something they must do. Some think, "At my age
being single is practically a failure." They treat dating like a requirement as if it were a qualification on a resume for job hunting. But if you see it that way wouldn't it be better to just live your life without the unnecessary stress?

title: 💔7년 연애의 참견 짬바로 맛깔난 연애 고민 상담 끼려왔습니다 | 주우재의 연애 고민상담소
text:
being single is practically a failure." They treat dating like a requirement as if it were a qualification on a resume for job hunting. But if you see it that way wouldn't it be better to just live your life without the unnecessary stress? That way, you're happy
and your partner is happy too. There's no universal rule for
how many dates per week are appropriate. [Only speaking facts]
It's not like some global organization has set a standard. It all depends on who you're dating and what kind of balance you can create together. [There is no fixed standard]
There's no set rule for this. [Next submission] "What are your thoughts on splitting the bill
evenly?" This is from "Samsun." "When we go on dates, we split the bill evenly
but my partner eats way more than I do. They have a bigger build and usually
eat enough for two or three people. Plus, they have expensive taste, so instead of just grilling meat ourselves, we end up
going to places where they cook it for us [I'm a college student living off]
which makes our food expenses skyrocket. [my part-time job earnings]
Even if I cut back on everything else just one meal together makes it hard to manage
my budget, and it's becoming really stressful. I'd like to keep my meal expenses
to around 10,000 to 20,000 won but seeing myself spend 40,000 won
per person is just too much." So it sounds like they have a shared
dating fund that they both contribute to. "I don't know how to tell them to pay more. When I need to save money I suggest, 'Let's just grab a simple burger' or 'I have plans for dinner, so
let's just go to a cafe beforehand.' But even then, the burger isn't
just any regular burger. It's some fancy, dark-colored bun burger
that costs over 10,000 won." Is that… Burger King? [A solid addition]
"Plus they add a junior-sized one on the side. And at cafes, they order a frappuccino
and some kind of pastry on top of that. Honestly, this whole bill-splitting thing is
making me feel like I need to break up. I'm an adult. I can't be asking my parents
for money just to go on dates. Is this a valid reason to break up? How should I even bring it up? Is this just how relationships are? Do people normally starve themselves
on regular days just so they can splurge on dates? This whole food situation is
stressing me out so much that I feel like I could just never date again." Here's how I see it. Everyone has different ways of handling money. Ha… But honestly
I've always found the whole even-split

title: 💔7년 연애의 참견 짬바로 맛깔난 연애 고민 상담 끼려왔습니다 | 주우재의 연애 고민상담소
text:
rge on dates? This whole food situation is
stressing me out so much that I feel like I could just never date again." Here's how I see it. Everyone has different ways of handling money. Ha… But honestly
I've always found the whole even-split system uncomfortable. It's just my personal opinion but whether it's with friends or in a relationship I've always had a strong dislike for 1/N splits. In fact, I think this is the quickest way to create resentment. For example sometimes people take turns paying. Let's say I buy an expensive meal and next time
the other person just buys me coffee. That might feel unfair like, "I bought dinner and all they got me was a cheap coffee?" But in that situation you can't calculate things down to the smallest detail. Sure, that might be frustrating but when you go full-on 1/N things get even messier.
You start keeping track of everything. [1/N turns everything into a calculation]
You find yourself counting pieces of pork. Because this is about just this one meal and not over time, you suddenly start noticing "I only ate this much but they ate that much." [It creates situations]
This only leads to more stressful [where you can't help but calculate]
and frustrating situations. That's why, when I go out
with younger friends or juniors I usually just pay for everything. It's because when I was younger my seniors used to cover my meals and I feel like I should pass that on. Of course you could say "Well, you can afford it now" which is true. But even when I was in my twenties
back in my college days I had the mindset that I should pay more in relationships. And here's what happens
when you take that approach. Some partners take it completely for granted. If that happens
I naturally start losing feelings. And it's not just about the money
it reveals their character in other areas too. Eventually, I lose affection
for her, and we break up. But if, on the other hand I have a partner who, every time I pay instinctively reaches for her wallet [That kind of action] even if she doesn't actually end up paying it changes everything. If her actions feel sincere
and not just performative that small effort alone makes a huge difference. It's all about attitude. If I always pay and the other person just casually
walks out of the

title: 💔7년 연애의 참견 짬바로 맛깔난 연애 고민 상담 끼려왔습니다 | 주우재의 연애 고민상담소
text:
ctually end up paying it changes everything. If her actions feel sincere
and not just performative that small effort alone makes a huge difference. It's all about attitude. If I always pay and the other person just casually
walks out of the restaurant without even acknowledging it [Conclusion]
it starts feeling way too one-sided. And once the idea of me
always paying becomes an expectation the relationship starts to lose its warmth and falls apart. [Looking at this submission]
Because of the even-split system every meal now feels like a mental battle. You're probably so focused on how much they're eating [Eating together just becomes annoying]
that you don't even taste your own food. This whole situation is just eating away at you. The problem is, this even-split system has already been set in stone. It's how your relationship operates. If it's stressing you out to the point where you're sending in a submission I think you should just break up. That being said, if you feel comfortable enough to have a conversation and say "Let's stop splitting everything evenly. If I pay for a meal you can just cover coffee next time" and if they're open to that then maybe try adjusting things first. In the end what matters most in relationships is mental health. Why do we date? To find comfort to have someone we can lean on to experience love and care. So why put yourself through something
that's harming your mental well-being? Since I believe that mental health is
the most important factor in a relationship I suggest you try to realign things. But if you think nothing will change break up immediately. [Next submission] This comes from "MangoSago."
"Dating an ISTP." Oh, that sounds happy. "Hello. I'm a female subscriber
in my late twenties. I'm currently dating
an ISTP boyfriend, just like you. At first, I was drawn to
how he approached things carefully stayed calm, and didn't have extreme mood swings.
That's what made me like him and start a relationship. But after dating for a while, I realized that whenever I want emotional support
he only responds with logical answers. For example, when I get nervous
about flying and say 'The plane is moving now' he just says, 'Yeah, it's taking off
so of course it's moving.' Or if I say 'Isn't it freezing today?' he'll reply, 'Yeah, the weather forecast
said it would be cold. You should've worn a thicker coat.' Every time I hear these responses, I feel
annoyed, disappointed, and speechless. How can I get him to be more empathetic? [For reference, I'm an INFP]
Or is this just something I have to deal with? Since you're also an ISTP
I'd really like to hear your advice!" Isn't there something simple he could do? For example, when you say
"The plane is moving now" instead of just replying
"Yeah, it's taking off, so of course it's moving" maybe he could hold your hand? That's what I would do. Or "Yeah, the weather forecast
said it would be cold. You should've worn a thicker coat" he could also be unwrapping
his own scarf to give it to you. That's just how I am. [Hehe]
I don't know about other ISTPs but that's just me. To be completely honest I don't really see why this is upsetting. At the end of the day
isn't it about how you already have a set response you want to hear? I can almost guarantee that your boyfriend wasn't like this from the very beginning. I say this with confidence. What pushes people like us
even further in this direction is when we feel like every question has a predetermined correct answer.
When our partner keeps fishing for a specific response
we start getting more blunt. We can't help it. For people like us, the best fuel
for ruining a relationship is a conversation with a predetermined answer. Of course, if two people with
a similar mindset date and they both agree to exchange
specific phrases like "I say this, and you reply with this, okay?" "Now!" and that makes them happy [Haha] then great. But since you already know you and
your boyfriend have different styles just leave it be. Stop asking questions with a particular answer in mind. Just leave it be. If you do, you might actually
start seeing more warmth [Trust me on this]
and affection from him. But if, despite all that, you still can't stand it then honestly, you'd probably be happier finding someone with a strong "F" personality. Someone who will say
all the things you want to hear. That would probably make for
a much smoother and happier relationship. "An ex-boyfriend with the perfect looks
vs. A crush with the perfect personality." [Oh?]
So you're in a position where you get to choose. "My ex-boyfriend was exactly
my type in terms of appearance but his personality made
the relationship really difficult. He was avoidant and
not good at expressing affection. But he dressed well, was tall
and had a sophisticated sense of style. On the other hand
my current crush isn't my type physically. He dresses terribly, and his taste isn't refined. But his personality is incredibly stable
and he expresses affection well. They really feel like complete opposites. My ex and I dated for three years but we broke up because he was stressed about
job hunting. However, he left things open-ended saying he would come back for me when he became
successful, which has left me feeling conflicted." This guy isn't coming back. "My crush is truly a good person and I don't want to date him casually
only to break up later, so I'm even more torn. Please give me advice from a 'T' perspective." This is just like what I've said before. If someone is exactly your type in terms of looks but is completely incompatible
with you in personality that's pure hell. Do you know why? Because you realize you can't let go of them. [Pain]
You might think "Let's just fight one last time and end it" the moment you see their face, you think "Wow, will I ever find someone
this good-looking again?" So you can't let go. And that just leads to more clashes [Fight, then forgive because of his face]
more stress [Repeat the cycle]
and complete emotional burnout but you still can't let go because
you're too attached to that appearance. It's just an endless loop of misery. [Woo Jae has been there, too]
I've been through that. Looking back now, I have thoughts like in a Kim Dong Ryul song "If I could start over I'd be more accommodating, I'd do better." But that doesn't work. It's impossible. "If I could go back to who I was I'd adjust and things would work out, right?" No. [It's just not a match]
It's because you weren't compatible. No matter how attractive they are if the relationship doesn't work
you're better off letting go and finding someone else. But the reverse is also true.
If someone really isn't your type visually [Same thing]
that's a problem, too. No matter how great their personality is this is someone you'll have to
see every single day. If they're far from your style it can be difficult to develop romantic feelings. And if you choose them solely
because your personalities and conversations match well [Just stay friends]
then why not just be friends? What's the point of being
in a romantic relationship? You gave me two options but if you look around I'm sure you'll find people who have both decent looks
and a personality that aligns with yours. And honestly, if you even have
the luxury of choosing that means you're probably popular. You'll have no problem finding someone. And let me give you a little dose of reality. Exes who break up due to job search stress almost always leave some kind of vague
"I'll come back for you" message. [He was just trying to sound nice while leaving]
Because no one wants to be the bad guy when ending a relationship. Let me just say this. He is never coming back. "My girlfriend put my gifts up for sale
on a secondhand platform." [Bursts out laughing] Oh wow. Did this really happen? This story comes from "Teddy." "Hello, I'm a 31-year-old man. I'm writing because I'm facing a situation
I've never experienced before. My girlfriend and I have been
dating for eight months. Out of all the people I've met in my life she is by far the most active user
of secondhand platforms like Karrot Market. At first, I didn't think much of it but over time, I got curious "What was she selling so often?" That simple curiosity led me to discover something I wish I had never seen. I enjoy giving gifts to my girlfriend so I've given her presents from time to time. Sometimes, I'd pick brands or designs
that aren't commonly used. But then I found out that some of those gifts were listed for sale on a secondhand platform. The username on the platform
the meeting location for transactions everything pointed to my girlfriend's neighborhood so I wanted to deny it
but there was no doubt. It was her. After finding out, I was so shocked that I didn't know what to do. It's been about three weeks
since I discovered this but I still haven't confronted her about it. Since then, I can't bring myself
to give her anything and every time I see her
I can't stop thinking about it. I'd appreciate it if Woo Jae could share
some insight into my girlfriend's mindset and what I should do going forward." I've been on dating shows for seven years and there's something I always say. It applies to every relationship story I hear. When something is excessive, avoid it.
That's a phrase I always stick to. If something crosses the boundary of common sense steer clear. Selling secondhand items? Sure, it's great. If you have unused items you can convert them into cash someone else can make good use of them you can earn some extra money and it helps declutter your space. It's a win-win. But listing a gift
your boyfriend gave you for sale? [Shakes head]
I can't understand that. [Hear me out]
When you give a gift you do it because you were thinking
about that person while preparing it. She knows that feeling. Everyone has felt it before, including me. It's an overwhelming emotion. And while expecting a huge reaction when giving a gift isn't necessarily right at the very least, you want to feel
that the other person appreciates it. [Gifting because it makes me happy]
Sometimes, we even give gifts for our own emotions for the joy of giving. But knowing all of that she still took your gift and turned it into cash? [Can't comprehend]
I just can't wrap my head around it. This crosses the line of excess. It's behavior that falls outside
the bounds of common sense so you should avoid this. [Look]
You already said in your submission that you no longer feel like giving her gifts.
That means the trust that's essential in a relationship
has already started to crack. [Once trust is broken]
The foundation is already breaking [There's nothing you can do]
and at this point, it's just a matter of time. [Moving on to the next submission] "I really want to be in a relationship." "I'm a third-year university student and I really want to be in a relationship." I'm already feeling emotional. "Actually, come to think of it
maybe I don't want it that badly." Oh? Now I'm even more emotional. "I do enjoy being alone but seeing all my friends in relationships makes me feel even lonelier. Lately, my friends have been
talking about wanting to get married which has made me start
thinking about marriage, too. When I consider pregnancy, childbirth, and raising kids I feel like it would be best for my body to give birth in my late twenties to early thirties. If that's the case, I should start
dating in my mid-twenties and get married in my late twenties but when I think about it
I don't have much time left. People say you should meet
a lot of people to find the right one but I have no idea where to meet them. I used to think that if I couldn't find the right person
I just wouldn't get married. But now, I feel like I definitely
want to get married and have kids so if I'm going to do it
wouldn't it be better to start soon? That thought has made me a little anxious. Looking back at my past relationships I enjoyed the talking stage but once we actually started dating
I lost interest quickly and couldn't stay in a relationship for long.
That worries me even more. What do you think about marriage? As a fan of Woo Jae I always think you should
get married and have kids soon so I decided to send in my story." Ah, well I'll handle my own situation. This is something I often talk about after
doing a relationship program for seven years. When it comes to marriage
people tend to fixate on timelines. Marriage in your late twenties to early thirties. [The dreaded 9]
The number 9 is intimidating. 29. Many people rush into marriage at 29 because they feel anxious about turning 30. But rushing into marriage like that increases the risk of making poor choices. So I don't think you should decide on marriage on age or time constraints. You've already set a timeline [A solo plan]
which means you need to start dating now. But if you force things to fit a schedule you'll end up trying to assemble pieces that don't naturally fit together. [Somewhat understandable, but…]
It's natural to feel anxious because you're not currently dating and time keeps passing. But first don't let external influences
dictate your decisions. Seeing others in relationships
or planning weddings shouldn't make you feel left behind. That kind of pressure often results in poor decisions. Ultimately it's about action. You wrote, "I enjoyed the talking stage
but once we actually started dating I lost interest quickly and
couldn't stay in a relationship for long." That means you haven't found someone who truly captures your interest. So, stop thinking about marriage and kids for now. [Prioritize dating first]
Just focus on dating first. Go places where you can meet people. "Where are those places?" you might ask. Anywhere. Go out, socialize accept introductions. If you actively put yourself out there you'll eventually meet someone who makes you think [Heart pounding]
"Wait, why am I feeling this way? Why am I still so interested?" That's when you know it's right. If you obsess over timelines and plans it'll feel like failure [Brain: Failure detected]
when things don't align perfectly [Me: Despair…]
which leads to more frustration. The moment you start planning everything down to the last detail things will start falling apart. Instead, throw yourself into the dating scene and embrace the chaos. The answers will come naturally. "How to get back together."
This story comes from "Ppyororyororyong." "Hello, Woo Jae. Lately, my dating troubles
have turned me into 'any sheep.' I just can't get 'any sleep.'" Oh, that was good. I read that really well, right? "Please, just tell me what to do. Early last year, I was dumped out of the blue by my boyfriend of about 100 days. Before and after dating him
I've had other boyfriends but I've never met anyone who was
as compatible with me as he was. Even when we broke up, he mentioned about how we had never fought
and had a good relationship. I cried and begged him to stay but he ended things, saying
I didn't fit into his vision of marriage. Then, months later, he suddenly contacted me again saying he missed me and
wanted to stay in touch as friends. What is going on in his head? I really liked him so I agreed to be friends for now but I'm not sure if I should cut ties
or try to get back together. What do you even say to someone who doesn't see you as marriage material
but still wants to keep in touch? Thank you for reading
and I appreciate your advice." When a guy reaches out after a breakup asking to stay friends there are really only two reasons. Either he doesn't have romantic feelings anymore but still sees you as someone who understands him and helps him in certain ways or… Well… he misses something else entirely. It's one of those two. Either way, neither option meets your expectations so, to put it bluntly, this isn't going to work. [0% chance]
This isn't going anywhere. In cases like this the best move is to let go and find someone else. The best approach is to be cold to him. He said he wants to stay friends, right? I know it's tough. I know your heart isn't going to listen to logic. I also know that the moment
he's even slightly kind to you [Oh my] your heart is going to race. But as I always say the only way to even slightly increase
the chances of rekindling something is to act indifferent. You need to show him, "I'm fine without you. I don't need you in my life." That's the only way to make him feel even
a little anxious about losing you. [What not to do]
"Oh… So I can at least stay near you as a friend?" That kind of attitude will only make your chances drop to zero. Alright, let's take a look at one last story. This story comes from "Goaengyi." "How can I show interest in someone?" "Hello, I'm a freshman who just turned twenty. My concern is that I can't show
my interest when I like someone. I don't know how people start dating but I think a relationship begins
when one person expresses interest and if the other person reciprocates they gradually build a connection. But I can't show my feelings at all
when I like someone. The problem is, it's not just me
who thinks I don't show it neither the person I like
nor my friends have any idea who I have feelings for. So whenever I like someone I usually just admire them
from afar until it ends. Now that I'm starting college I want to get close to someone I like flirt a little, and make my feelings more obvious but I'm naturally introverted, not social and have very few friends, let alone male friends. I'm not good at getting close to guys in general. And if it's someone I really like
I find it even harder to start a conversation. At this rate, I feel like I won't be able to date at all in college. For reference, I'm also an ISTP like Joo Woo Jae. Woo Jae, how did you approach someone you liked and show your interest?" First, let's talk about the harsh truth. People usually know when someone likes them. You think you're not showing it but if you've ever talked to the person even once they probably picked up on it. The fact that you never got any feedback likely means
you were the only one interested. [Now, for the hopeful part]
This is purely a lack of experience. There's no special trick. It's all about experience. Think about when you first
ask someone for their number. Remember those old cringy lines "If you have coffee…
Can I… have a sip of your time?" Everyone stumbles at first. But then there are people
who've done it enough and say "Hey, do you have a boyfriend?" or "Wow, you're totally my type." Of course not everyone talks like that but the point is, everyone starts out awkward. If you never try you'll never gain experience and life will just pass you by. In college you'll have way more opportunities
than in high school. Get through a few embarrassing moments and don't let them break you. Of course, use common sense. And when you fail, take mental notes like "Oh, so that didn't work." [Don't be afraid]
Let yourself get rejected a few times let yourself fumble and eventually you'll start getting the hang of it. [You can do it]
You'll find your own style. You're defining yourself too rigidly. Like, "I'm just someone who doesn't show interest. People see me this way so I'll never be able to date." [Nothing comes without effort. Just go for it!]
But how can you expect results without effort? After trying enough times, you'll get used to it and one day, you'll find yourself
surprisingly smooth. When I like someone I'm a total smooth talker. It's not that hard. [You can do it, too]
"Goaengyi," you can do it. You just need time to build experience. Once ISTPs get the hang of it they're experts at subtly showing interest while pretending not to. Once you've mastered it you'll drive guys crazy. [Become the female version of Woo Jae, ISTP]
Guys are going to go crazy for you. [Today's Joo WooJae] So, we've gone through
some relationship concerns today. Honestly as you've seen from watching me I don't usually care much
about other people's business and I don't like getting involved. But since you all send in your stories
and set the stage for this I figure I might as well treat it like
it's my own problem while answering. That's why I try to be as sincere
as possible when I give advice. That said, I know my lack of empathy
or certain comments that "F" types might find too harsh could come through at times. But just remember, these are simply my personal thoughts in the moment. Don't take them as some absolute truth. Just enjoy this content
as lighthearted life advice. Let's wrap up with an outfit introduction. The half-duffle coat I'm wearing is from Sacai. [OUTER SACAI]
It has a half-length cut with duffle coat details making it look really cute. You know, that kind of look [Adorable Half-Coat]
that supposedly drives certain people crazy. [PANTS FRAGE]
As for the pants I wore denim from a new brand called Frage. I had previously worn
their leather jacket in another video and they sent me these jeans to try. They have a deep blue wash
with a slightly retro feel subtle fading details and a long loose silhouette. [SHOES GREEN DOOR]
The shoes are deep brown loafers from Green Door. Next time, I'll be back
with another round of advice. Next time, I think I should
bring someone else along for this. If I only share my own perspective things could get too one-sided. Maybe Orbit or someone else I can have
a good conversation with. See you next time.
